scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the contemporary practice of Athenian magistrates and laws in the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Athenian Constitution was likely written around -330 near the end of Aristotle's life; it captures contemporary Athenian practice.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is documenting the contemporary military enrollment procedures of the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text reflects the administrative procedures of the Council of Five Hundred in Athens at the time of writing, circa -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the Athenian Council's welfare system for disabled citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This state allowance was an active institutional practice in Athens during Aristotle's residence in the mid-4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the duties of the Astynomi (City Commissioners) in the Athenian polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle reports on the specific price controls enforced by current Athenian magistrates during his floruit.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the mandatory service of 60-year-old Athenian citizens as public arbitrators.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The system of public arbitration described was a contemporary feature of the late 4th-century Athenian legal system.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule details the specific penalties for Athenian magistrates as part of their accountability to the state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle outlines the judicial oversight mechanisms currently in place in Athens during the mid-330s BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'dokimasia' (examination) described is a central feature of identifying eligible leaders for the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The religious and ancestral requirements for the Nine Archons were active legal standards in Aristotle's time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The selection of Choregi from the wealthiest citizens is a classic Athenian liturgical (public service) institution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly updates the reader on current practice ('but now the tribes provide...') as of his writing in -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The eligibility and exemption rules for choregraphic service refer to the laws of the Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The age limit for the choregus of a boys' chorus was a contemporary legal detail in 4th-century Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Palladium court was a specific Athenian judicial body for cases of non-citizen and slave homicide.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes the jurisdictional divisions of the Athenian legal system as they functioned in his own day.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text identifies the protections afforded to metics (resident aliens) within the Athenian legal framework.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The jurisdictional duties of the King Archon and the Palladium court are documented as current Athenian law.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Polemarch was a specific Athenian magistrate whose role was to represent non-citizens in court.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The legal parity of aliens overseen by the Polemarch was an active feature of the Athenian democracy in -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes legal restrictions on slaves within the social and judicial hierarchy of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle's account of the Thesmothetae and private suits reflects the contemporary 4th-century Athenian legal code.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author explicitly frames this as a historical rule ('Formerly') regarding sacred olives and the Areopagus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes a past legal practice that had been modernized/superseded relative to his own 4th-century BCE context.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the current method of electing the ten Generals (Strategi) from the whole citizen body.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author contrasts older tribal methods with the 'now' of the contemporary Athenian constitution in -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The payment for assembly attendance is a hallmark of the Radical/Classical Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The specific pay rates (drachma, nine obols) represent the contemporary economic state of the Athenian polis circa -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The provision of jury and council pay was a defining feature of the day-to-day governance in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle lists these daily allowances as the active remuneration system in his own time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The age and status requirements for jurors describe the composition of the Heliaia in contemporary Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: These qualifications were part of the active court regulations described by Aristotle in the late 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of state debtors from civic participation is a core legal doctrine of the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle details the active 'information' (endeixis) procedure used to punish unqualified jurors in -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rules governing the tenure of military versus civil offices are central to the Athenian political structure.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This differentiation in service terms was the operational standard of the Athenian democracy at the time of writing.
